title: Police Manual - Impaired Driving
content:
POLICE MANUAL - IMPAIRED DRIVING

Land Transport Act 1998
Land Transport (Drug and Alcohol) Legislation

=== ALCOHOL IMPAIRMENT ===

Compulsory Breath Testing (CBT):
- Police may conduct random breath testing at checkpoints
- No reasonable grounds required at checkpoints
- Individual stops require suspicion of alcohol/drug impairment
- Refusal to submit to breath test is an offence

Breath Testing Procedure:
1. Screening test (passive or active)
2. If positive, evidential breath test OR blood test
3. 15-minute observation period before evidential test
4. Right to consult lawyer AFTER procedure completed
5. Right to independent blood test

Alcohol Limits:
- Under 20 years: ZERO alcohol (0 mcg breath / 0 mg blood)
- 20 years and over: 250 mcg per litre breath / 50 mg per 100ml blood
- Excess breath alcohol: 400 mcg / 80 mg
- Prior conviction: Lower limits may apply

Evidential Breath Testing:
- Must use approved evidential breath analysing device
- Must observe driver for 15 minutes before test
- No food, drink, smoking, vomiting during observation
- Two samples required (lower reading used)
- Printout provided to driver
- Blood test offered if over limit

Blood Tests for Alcohol:
- Medical practitioner or qualified person must take sample
- Driver may request independent blood test
- Sample divided into two portions
- Police portion analysed
- Driver portion available for independent analysis
- Chain of custody critical

=== DRUG IMPAIRMENT ===

Compulsory Impairment Test (CIT):
- Physical coordination test (walk and turn, one leg stand, finger to nose)
- Eye examination (pupil size, reaction to light)
- Divided attention tests
- Must be conducted by trained officer
- Video recording recommended

Blood Tests for Drugs:
- Zero tolerance for specified drugs:
  * Cannabis (THC)
  * Methamphetamine
  * Cocaine
  * MDMA (Ecstasy)
  * Other Class A drugs
- Blood sample taken by medical practitioner
- Laboratory analysis for active impairment
- Prescription drug defence available (but not if impaired)

Prescription Drugs:
- Legal to drive with prescription medication IF not impaired
- Offence to drive while impaired by ANY substance
- Medical defence requires proof drug taken as prescribed
- Warning labels on medication

=== PROCEDURES ===

Checkpoint Procedures:
- Must be operationally justified
- Must be safe (lighting, traffic control)
- Random selection of vehicles
- Quick screening (few seconds per vehicle)
- Secondary testing area for positive screens

Individual Stop Procedures:
- Reasonable grounds required (observation of impairment)
- Signs: erratic driving, smell of alcohol, bloodshot eyes, slurred speech
- Standard impairment tests
- Decision: arrest, summons, or warn

Rights of Driver:
- Right to consult lawyer (AFTER procedure)
- Right to independent blood test
- Right to copy of results
- Right to know reason for stop
- Right to silence (but refusal to provide sample is offence)

=== OFFENCES ===

Drink Driving Offences:
- Driving with excess breath/blood alcohol
- Driving under influence of alcohol (impairment)
- Refusal to provide breath sample
- Refusal to provide blood sample
- Allowing impaired person to drive

Drug Driving Offences:
- Driving while impaired by drug
- Driving with qualifying drug in blood
- Refusal to provide blood sample
- Allowing impaired person to drive

Penalties:
- Licence disqualification (mandatory minimums)
- Fines (up to $6,000)
- Imprisonment (up to 3 months first offence, 2 years repeat)
- Alcohol interlock device (repeat offenders)
- Zero alcohol licence
- Vehicle confiscation (third offence)

Aggravating Factors:
- High reading (double limit or more)
- Crash causing injury/death
- Previous convictions
- Passengers in vehicle
- Fleeing from Police

=== MEDICAL ASSESSMENT ===

Doctor's Assessment:
- Medical practitioner may examine driver
- Assess fitness to drive
- Determine if medical condition contributed
- Document injuries (post-crash)
- Blood sample authorisation

Blood-Borne Viruses:
- Risk to officers from blood samples
- Universal precautions required
- Gloves mandatory
- Safe disposal of sharps
- Post-exposure prophylaxis if exposed

=== EVIDENCE ===

Evidence Required:
- Certificate of breath analysis (evidential device)
- Blood analysis certificate
- Chain of custody documentation
- Officer observations
- Video footage (if available)
- Medical records (if applicable)

Challenging Evidence:
- Device calibration and maintenance records
- Officer training records
- Proper procedure followed
- Rights explained
- Time limits (blood sample within 3 hours of driving)

=== DEFENCES ===

Available Defences:
- Not driving or in charge
- Not on road or road-related area
- Alcohol consumed AFTER driving (defence to excess, not impairment)
- Proper testing not conducted
- Medical condition affecting reading
- Device malfunction
- Procedural defects

Alcohol Consumed After Driving:
- Must prove drinking after stopped driving
- Does not apply to impairment charge
- May reduce reading but not eliminate liability

=== SPECIAL CIRCUMSTANCES ===

Crash Involvement:
- Blood sample compulsory if serious injury/death
- Hospital procedures
- Medical priority
- Evidence preservation
- Time critical

Unconscious Driver:
- Blood sample may be taken without consent (serious injury/death)
- Medical practitioner approval
- Life-threatening situation

Workplace Testing:
- Different thresholds for commercial drivers
- Zero tolerance for some categories
- Post-crash testing
- Random testing at some workplaces

Related Legislation:
- Land Transport Act 1998
- Land Transport (Road User) Rule 2004
- Land Transport (Drugs and Alcohol) Legislation
- Health and Safety at Work Act 2015
key_procedures: Observe driver for signs of impairment
Conduct compulsory breath or impairment test
Follow 15-minute observation period for evidential tests
Offer blood test if over limit
Arrange safe transport if driver unfit
